input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, featuring a permanent magnet synchronous motor, gear transmission, cycloidal gear, and Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, designed for high-precision industrial manufacturing applications where accurate motion control and real-time monitoring are critical.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for system modeling across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs are used for component & connector perspectives. Behavior modeling is achieved via MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, integrated with MathWorks Simulink for simulation, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed behavior specification. Methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT handle system complexity and stakeholder integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system employs a Digital Shadow Reference Model defined as a UML class diagram (Fig. 1) and a Concrete Digital Shadow Model (Fig. 5), integrating components such as motor, gearbox, and sensors. It utilizes architecture patterns including SysML and UML for structural modeling, and implements Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized digital representation and interoperability.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is achieved through MQTT protocol for real-time sensor data streaming, Asset Administration Shells (AAS) ensuring interoperability across platforms, and connectivity to the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller for industrial control system communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies using SysML for structural modeling (e.g., component & connector perspectives via IBDs), functional and logical architecture descriptions, and behavioral modeling with state machines. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML are utilized to define system components, interfaces, and interactions. Analytical models are implemented through MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose programming languages like C++ or Java. Data originates from physical systems via one-way digital shadow data flow, with aggregation and abstraction for specific purposes, integrating heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives through comprehensive MBSE frameworks.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data is streamed from physical components to the digital system via MQTT protocol.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for communication between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle phases of the Digital Twin encompass development stages defined by Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE), where modeling is structured across abstraction levels including requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers. The methodology for deriving Digital Shadows (as part of the Twin development process) includes four specific steps: 1) Problem identification, 2) Asset and model analysis, 3) Use or creation of calculations/models, and 4) Data identification. These steps are supported by methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT, which manage complexity through structured abstraction and integration of heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives during the development phase.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process utilizes SysML for system architecture modeling, Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) like ACME, AADL, and MontiArc for component & connector perspectives, and integrates simulation tools such as MathWorks Simulink along with general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed behavior specification. This approach addresses system complexity through structured abstraction levels and stakeholder integration.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with industry standards includes the use of Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for structured asset information modeling, SysML for system architecture description, and MQTT protocol for lightweight publish-subscribe messaging in data streaming, ensuring interoperability and adherence to industrial communication protocols.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The safety cell of the robot restricts the maximum working range to [-90°, 90°].

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately identifies the specific robot model (MABI MAX 100), its configuration (six axes), and detailed hardware components (motor type, gears, controller) found in the source. It also correctly places the system in the context of high-precision manufacturing. Supported by quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly mentions specific services provided by the digital shadow, such as data visualization, friction identification, and improving motion accuracy. Because evidence exists in the text, the claim that it is missing is incorrect. Quotes: 'live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard', 'enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', 'improve robot motion accuracy'.

The extraction is highly specific and directly supported by Section II.A of the source documents. It accurately lists the use of 'Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)' and 'SysML' for modeling across 'requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer'. It correctly enumerates methodologies 'e.g., RFLP..., OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT' and 'Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc'. It also correctly identifies behavior modeling via 'MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML v1.7' and simulation tools like 'MathWorks Simulink'.

However, it does not provide specific technical details regarding synchronization frequencies, specific latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined in the characteristic description. Therefore, the claim 'Not in Document' is accurate regarding the specific technical parameters.

The extraction accurately summarizes the system's architecture, referencing the specific models (UML, SysML), the components (motor, gearbox, sensors), and standards (AAS) defined in the text. It is highly specific and grounded in the provided sources.
